name: Red & Blue Comparative
description: A crisp comparative-analysis system with bold editorial type, red and blue data coding, and structured insight cards.
style_guide:
# Red & Blue Comparative

## Visual concept

This is an English-language executive comparison style for market, country, competitor, and sector analysis. It should feel analytical, contemporary, and editorial: generous white space, very bold dark headlines, restrained geometric decoration, and a clear two-sided red-versus-blue data language. Every page should make the comparison legible within a few seconds and then reward closer reading with evidence and implications.

## Canvas, grid, and safe area

- Use a fixed `1280 × 720 px` canvas in a 16:9 ratio.
- Keep a `60–72 px` safe margin on every edge. No headline, chart label, or conclusion card may touch the boundary.
- Use a 12-column grid with `20–24 px` gutters. Typical layouts are 6/6 comparison panels, 7/5 chart-plus-insight panels, and a 3-column or 2×3 card grid.
- Keep a large title band at the top: title on the left, optional English section label on the right. A subtitle or one-sentence framing statement sits directly below the title.
- Align all major cards and chart frames to shared vertical edges. Do not create decorative floating elements that break the grid.
- Reserve the bottom `32–44 px` for a quiet source line, date, or page number.

## Color system

### Core colors

- Ink: `#252A2D`. Use for primary headlines, body emphasis, table labels, and major numbers. Never use pure black.
- Side A red: `#FF4B5C`. Use for the first entity, positive emphasis, primary comparison series, and left-side panel accents.
- Side B blue: `#3587D8`. Use for the second entity, comparison series, right-side panel accents, and secondary emphasis.
- Structural purple: `#5138A5`. Use for section words in mixed-color headlines, category accents, and strategic conclusions. It is a structural accent, not a third entity.

### Supporting colors

- Soft red: `#FFF0F1`. Use for Side A cards, icon backgrounds, and light callout fills.
- Soft blue: `#EEF5FD`. Use for Side B cards, icon backgrounds, and light callout fills.
- Lavender wash: `#F3F1FA`. Use for a conclusion card or low-contrast structural panel.
- Cool gray: `#687177`. Use for subtitles, body copy, axis labels, and metadata.
- Grid gray: `#E8EBEF`. Use for chart grids, separators, and card borders.
- Neutral panel: `#F7F8FA`. Use sparingly behind KPI rows or comparison details.

### Usage rules

- Red and blue are semantic: red always means Side A and blue always means Side B. Do not swap them from page to page.
- Use purple for structure or a shared conclusion; never use it as a third comparison category without an explicit legend.
- Keep the page mostly white. Strong colors should appear in headlines, chart series, card rails, icons, and selected numbers rather than as large background blocks.
- A chart may use teal, gray, or other neutral category colors only when the data requires more than two categories; keep those colors muted and explain them in a legend.

## Typography

- Primary font: `Inter` or `Arial`; fallback: `Helvetica Neue, system-ui, sans-serif`.
- Use heavy, compact sans-serif typography for headlines. Headlines may intentionally mix Ink and Structural purple, with the phrase after a colon or the key qualifier in purple.
- Cover title: `58–82 px`, weight `800–900`, line-height `0.98–1.05`. Keep the title to two lines or fewer.
- Page title: `42–52 px`, weight `800–900`, line-height `1.05`.
- Subtitle or framing sentence: `19–24 px`, weight `400–500`, line-height `1.4`.
- KPI number: `48–76 px`, weight `800–900`; entity-specific values use Side A red or Side B blue.
- Card title: `20–26 px`, weight `700–800`.
- Body copy: `16–19 px`, weight `400–500`, line-height `1.45–1.6`; keep paragraphs short.
- Chart title: `18–22 px`, weight `700–800`; labels and legends `13–16 px`.
- Sources, dates, and footnotes: `11–13 px`, weight `400–600`, in Cool gray.
- Use uppercase sparingly for section labels and legends. Keep normal prose in sentence case.

## Layout and component language

- Cards use white backgrounds, `1 px` Grid gray borders, `12–18 px` corner radius, and a barely visible shadow such as `0 5px 18px rgba(37,42,45,0.06)`.
- Comparison cards use a `5–7 px` top rail: Side A red on the first entity, Side B blue on the second.
- Entity headings pair a simple solid icon or colored dot with the entity name. Use matching soft-red or soft-blue icon backgrounds.
- KPI rows use a neutral or very light tinted panel with a large number, a concise label, and one short implication.
- Conclusion cards use Lavender wash or Neutral panel, a purple heading, and one explicit takeaway sentence.
- Use geometric background decoration only as very low-opacity circles, rings, or corner washes. It must remain behind the content and never compete with a chart.

## Charts and data

- Prefer horizontal comparisons, grouped bars, stacked bars, dot plots, and two-series line charts. Use donut charts only for simple composition breakdowns with direct labels.
- Side A series uses `#FF4B5C`; Side B series uses `#3587D8`. Use matching light fills for secondary states.
- Keep chart frames white with a light border or no border. Grid lines are `#E8EBEF`, `1 px`; remove heavy axis lines and unnecessary tick marks.
- Directly label important values and include units, time period, estimate markers, and data source. Do not make the audience infer the conclusion from color alone.
- A chart should answer one question. Pair it with a right-side insight stack or a bottom conclusion card rather than adding unrelated charts.
- Use teal, gray, and purple only for within-entity categories such as age bands or sectors; define all categories in a compact legend.

## Page variations

- Cover: large centered or left-aligned comparison title, a short English subtitle, a restrained red/blue legend, and soft geometric circles in the corners.
- Comparison overview: a short framing statement followed by three to six equal insight cards in a clean grid.
- Economic or market size page: one dominant chart on the left and a stacked KPI/implication rail on the right.
- Sector breakdown page: two equal entity panels with matching donut or stacked charts and a short conclusion strip inside each panel.
- Snapshot page: two equal entity columns with one hero number and three compact indicator rows per side.
- Distribution page: one large horizontal stacked chart and two entity-specific insight cards, followed by one purple key-conclusion card.

## Imagery and icons

- Prefer clean analytical diagrams, maps, flags, people, or products that clarify the comparison. Avoid generic lifestyle stock images.
- Use one consistent line or solid icon family. Icons should be simple, legible at `20–28 px`, and colored by entity or by the shared purple structure.
- Images and diagrams should sit inside the established grid with generous white margins. Do not apply dark overlays, cinematic filters, or decorative cropping.

## Avoid

- Do not use gradients in text, chart fills, or large backgrounds.
- Do not use a third saturated competitor color without a legend and a clear semantic reason.
- Do not use dense paragraphs, centered long-form body text, 3D charts, thick borders, glossy effects, or oversized decorative circles.
- Do not let red and blue become interchangeable. The audience must understand the two-sided encoding immediately.
- Do not place more than one primary chart or one primary conclusion on a page.
